planting.note: Plant corms 3-4 inches deep in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in fall for spring flowering; Byzantine gladiolus is a spring-blooming, fully hardy species planted in fall — completely different timing and hardiness from the tender summer gladiolus. In zones 6-9 the corms overwinter in the ground and naturalize freely, multiplying by offsets. Excellent drainage is essential; corms rot in wet soils.

directions: Plant corms 3-4 inches deep in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun in fall; Byzantine gladiolus is a world apart from the tender, summer-blooming garden gladiolus — it is planted in fall, blooms in late spring (May-June), and is fully cold-hardy to zone 5, naturalizing in well-drained conditions without any annual lifting or replanting. The flowers are a vivid, intense magenta-pink with paler centers — an unusual, saturated fuchsia-magenta color carried elegantly on graceful, arching stems with up to 20 florets per spike. The plant increases freely by cormlets to form naturalized colonies in appropriate soils, creating large drifts of spring color over time. Native to the western Mediterranean — Spain, North Africa, Turkey — it has been grown in European gardens since the 17th century and has naturalized on the Channel Islands and other mild Atlantic locations. A revelation for gardeners accustomed to lifting tender gladiolus annually; this species is trouble-free perennial. Combines beautifully with blue Camassia and late tulips for spring plantings.

planting.note: Plant corms 3-4 inches deep after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun for late-summer bloom; peacock orchid is tender (damaged below 28 degrees F) and in most zones is treated as an annual or the corms are lifted after frost and stored frost-free over winter. In zones 9-10 the corms can be left in the ground year-round in well-drained soil. The distinctive fragrance — sweet, vanilla-like, noticeable from several feet away — is most pronounced in the evening.

directions: Plant corms after last frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; peacock orchid is the most distinctive and fragrant member of the gladiolus genus — in fact it was long classified in a separate genus (Acidanthera bicolor var. murielae) because its flowers are so unlike typical garden gladiolus. The pure white, star-shaped flowers, each with a prominent, deep maroon-purple blotch at the center of each petal, are carried on tall, slender stems in August and September, nodding from the top of the stem in a loose, graceful arrangement quite unlike the stiffly formal, upright spikes of garden gladiolus. Most importantly, peacock orchid is fragrant — sweetly, distinctly scented, the fragrance carrying on the air in the evening — an unusual quality in the gladiolus family. The combined effect of the white-and-purple flowers, graceful drooping habit, and sweet fragrance has made it a favorite for cutting gardens and mixed borders. Native to the high-altitude Ethiopian Highlands of East Africa. Plant in quantity for the best effect.

planting.note: Divide established corms carefully in late spring when just barely in growth, or plant container-grown plants in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; dierama establishes slowly and resents disturbance — newly divided plants may take 2-3 years to flower. Plants grown from seed take 3-5 years to bloom. Give well-drained but consistently moist soil; dierama dislikes both drought and waterlogging.

directions: Plant in deep, moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; angel's fishing rod produces some of the most gracefully beautiful flowers of any garden bulb — long, arching, wiry, thread-like stems that sway and dance in the slightest breeze, each stem bearing small, pendant, bell-shaped flowers in rose-pink, magenta, purple, or deep wine-red that hang from the arching tips like fishing lines. The stems are extremely slender and flexible, arising 4-7 feet above a clump of evergreen, rush-like, narrow leaves, and the combination of movement in the breeze and the pendant, jewel-like flowers creates an effect of extraordinary lightness and grace. The plant grows from corms that slowly multiply to form clumps, and established specimens are long-lived and imposing. Native to the mountain grasslands of South Africa (the Drakensberg and surrounding highlands), it requires deep, fertile, well-drained but consistently moist soil. It resents disturbance and establishes slowly but, once settled, is a magnificent garden presence. Best near water features where its reflection adds to the effect.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; Mexican lily is damaged below about 15-20 degrees F and is best suited to zones 7-10. The spectacular flower scape, reaching 6 feet or more, appears once after the plant reaches maturity and then the main rosette dies, leaving offsets to continue the colony.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Mexican lily is a dramatic, architecturally imposing relative of the agave from the mountains of central Mexico, forming a bold rosette of broad, soft, blue-green, sword-shaped, yucca-like leaves that are spineless and non-lethal to touch — unlike true agaves. When mature, the plant produces one of the most spectacular flowering events in the succulent world: a massive, arching, coral-red to orange-red flower scape that bends dramatically under its own weight and grows to 5-7 feet, bearing drooping clusters of pendant, tubular, red-and-green flowers along its entire length. The coral-red scape itself is ornamental for weeks before and after the flowers open. The combination of dramatic arching scape, vivid coral-red color, and pendant green-tipped flowers is unlike anything else in hardy horticulture. After flowering, the main rosette dies but produces offsets at its base. An outstanding architectural specimen for warm, sheltered, well-drained gardens in zones 7-10, particularly effective with other bold-textured plants.

planting.note: Sow seeds in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline, clay or rocky soil in full sun in early spring or fall; wavyleaf coneflower grows in soils with a higher clay content than many echinaceas. It has narrower, more drooping (reflexed) ray petals than E. purpurea and blooms June-July, slightly earlier than E. purpurea. Cold stratification of seeds for 30-60 days improves germination rates.

directions: Sow seeds in lean, well-drained, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; wavyleaf coneflower is a distinct native species of the Ozark region of Missouri, Arkansas, Illinois, and Tennessee, readily distinguished from the common purple coneflower (E. purpurea) by its narrow, wavy-margined (undulate) leaves, more strongly reflexed (drooping) rose-pink to pale-pink ray petals, and earlier bloom time (June-July versus August for E. purpurea). The flowers are also notably fragrant, with a sweet, mild honey-like scent that contributes to its attractiveness to bumblebees, native bees, and butterflies. In the wild, it grows in rocky, often clay-based, glades and woodland openings in the Ozarks — adapted to heavier soils than some other echinaceas. As with all echinaceas, the persistent, spiny seed heads are highly attractive to goldfinches and other seed-eating birds in fall and winter. A genuinely distinct species worth growing alongside other echinaceas for its earlier bloom time, drooping ray style, and fragrance.
